clinical_note: Established 54-year-old patient seen today at Riverbend Pain Center in Portland, Oregon for follow-up of chronic low back pain. The patient self-reports using medical marijuana obtained from a local dispensary, primarily an evening THC-dominant edible, with partial relief of nocturnal pain and improved sleep. Dr. Alvarez counseled on starting low and going slow with dispensary gummies given variable potency. We discussed that daytime functioning remains limited and reviewed risks of combining cannabis with the current gabapentin regimen. The patient denied any recreational marijuana use or features of cannabis use disorder. Plan to continue conservative management and reassess opioid taper in eight weeks.

This is a 67-year-old patient with metastatic colon cancer presenting to Lakeshore Medical Group in Madison, Wisconsin with chemotherapy-induced nausea and poor appetite. After failing ondansetron, NP Chen and I discussed initiating dronabinol (Marinol or Syndros oral solution) 2.5 mg twice daily as an adjunct antiemetic and appetite stimulant. The patient previously tried over-the-counter CBD oil with minimal benefit and declined inhaled marijuana due to respiratory concerns. We reviewed the sedating effects of synthetic THC, also known as tetrahydrocannabinol, and arranged close monitoring. Family expressed understanding of the cannabinoid therapy plan.

Routine annual visit for a 41-year-old patient with fibromyalgia at Maplewood Family Practice in Asheville, North Carolina. The patient reports daily use of a sublingual CBD tincture purchased online and occasional hemp-derived delta-8 gummies, which contain delta-8-THC, for breakthrough pain. Dr. Okafor advised caution regarding unregulated delta-8 products and the lack of standardized cannabidiol dosing. We discussed that the patient is interested in trying medical marijuana but has not yet obtained a state card. Pain remains moderate; plan to add aquatic physical therapy and follow up in three months.

Postoperative day fourteen following total knee arthroplasty for a 60-year-old patient managed by the orthopedic service at Summit Surgical Hospital in Denver, Colorado. The patient disclosed vaping cannabis flower preoperatively and resumed using a THC vape pen for pain after discharge. Dr. Reyes counseled on wound healing risks associated with vaping and recommended transitioning to a non-inhaled cannabinoid such as an edible if continued use is desired, while cautioning against high-potency concentrate products. Surgical pain is improving and the patient has weaned to acetaminophen during the day. We reinforced that marijuana does not replace prescribed venous thromboembolism prophylaxis.

Initial physical therapy evaluation for chronic neck and shoulder pain in a 36-year-old patient referred to Cedar Hollow Rehabilitation in Boise, Idaho. The patient applies a topical CBD balm before home exercises and reports it reduces local soreness, and also uses a low-dose nightly THC tincture for sleep. Therapist Daniels documented that the patient avoids smoking and prefers cannabidiol-based products over hemp oil preparations. We outlined a six-week strengthening program and advised coordinating any medical marijuana adjustments with the referring physician. Tolerance to manual therapy was good today.

Follow-up after an emergency department visit for an acute migraine in a 29-year-old patient seen at Northgate Community Clinic in Tucson, Arizona. The patient reported using marijuana edibles and a CBN-containing sleep gummy at home before presenting, with a urine drug screen documented as UDS positive for THC as expected. Dr. Patel reviewed that cannabis intoxication may have contributed to symptom misattribution and counseled on hydration and trigger avoidance. We discussed that recreational marijuana from dispensary flower is not a reliable abortive for migraine. The patient was started on a triptan and scheduled for neurology referral.

A 48-year-old patient with diabetic peripheral neuropathy returns to Willow Creek Pain Associates in Sacramento, California for medication review. The patient has been prescribed nabiximols (Sativex) oromucosal spray through a clinical trial protocol and reports modest reduction in burning foot pain. NP Sullivan noted no significant intoxication and good adherence to the cannabinoid spray, which delivers balanced THC and CBD. We compared this to the patient's prior use of high-THC hashish, which caused unwanted cognitive effects. Plan to continue the nabiximols arm and reassess neuropathic scores at the next visit.

Palliative consultation for a 72-year-old patient with pancreatic cancer at Brightwater Cancer Institute in Charleston, South Carolina. The patient experiences refractory nausea and cachexia, and Dr. Nguyen is considering nabilone (Cesamet) given prior intolerance to dronabinol. The patient's daughter reports the family has been giving small amounts of a homemade cannabis tincture and a hash oil capsule, which we asked them to pause while we titrate prescribed cannabinoids. We counseled on the difference between regulated pharmaceutical THC and dispensary products. Comfort-focused goals of care were reaffirmed with the team.

Follow-up for chronic osteoarthritis pain in a 58-year-old patient at Harborview Medical Partners in Providence, Rhode Island. The patient successfully reduced ibuprofen use after starting a medical marijuana regimen, alternating a daytime CBD-dominant tincture with a small evening THC edible. Dr. Alvarez reviewed the patient's dispensary purchase log and reinforced safe storage away from grandchildren. We noted the patient declined a stronger flower product to avoid impairment while driving and screened negative for cannabis abuse. Blood pressure and renal function remain stable; continue current plan.

New patient evaluation for chronic pelvic pain in a 33-year-old patient referred to Stonebridge Pain & Spine in Minneapolis, Minnesota. The patient reports prior recreational cannabis use that they have been actively declining over the past year in favor of mindfulness and a low-dose CBD capsule. Dr. Kim documented that the patient is hesitant about THC due to a history of anxiety, with no evidence of cannabis dependence. We discussed cannabidiol's more favorable side-effect profile and the limited evidence for cannabinoids in pelvic pain. A multidisciplinary plan including pelvic floor therapy was initiated.

Six-month follow-up for a 9-year-old patient with Dravet syndrome managed at Glenford Children's Hospital in Columbus, Ohio. The patient continues Epidiolex (cannabidiol) oral solution with a meaningful reduction in seizure frequency reported by the parents. Dr. Marsh reviewed liver function tests, which remain within normal limits, and confirmed no use of any THC-containing or hemp products from outside sources. The family was counseled that pharmaceutical-grade cannabidiol differs substantially from over-the-counter CBD. Dosing was adjusted for weight gain and follow-up labs were ordered through Glenford Children's Hospital.

Discharge summary from a twelve-visit course of therapy for lumbar radiculopathy in a 45-year-old patient at Foothills Sports Medicine in Salt Lake City, Utah. The patient reported using a topical CBD cream and an occasional cannabis vape pen for flare-ups during the program. Therapist Brooks noted improved functional scores and encouraged tapering reliance on inhaled marijuana as activity tolerance increased. We documented the patient's interest in switching from vaping to an edible or tincture and referred this discussion back to Dr. Alvarez. Home exercise program reviewed and patient verbalized understanding.

Post-discharge follow-up for a 51-year-old patient evaluated for chest tightness, ultimately deemed musculoskeletal, at Rosewood Urgent Care in Albuquerque, New Mexico. The patient reported heavy daily use of delta-9 THC edibles and dispensary flower, with a urine drug screen positive for cannabinoids. NP Chen counseled on cannabinoid hyperemesis risk and the cardiovascular effects of high-dose marijuana, and noted features consistent with cannabis use disorder. We discussed gradually decreasing intake and provided resources for cessation support. The patient agreed to follow up with primary care and consider non-cannabis pain strategies.

Routine visit for a 63-year-old patient with chronic low back pain and prior lumbar fusion at Riverbend Pain Center in Portland, Oregon. The patient holds a state medical marijuana (MMJ) card and uses a balanced THC and CBD tincture, having discontinued smoking hashish several years ago. Dr. Reyes reviewed the urine drug screen showing expected THC and counseled on continued avoidance of combustible products. We noted the patient declined an increase in opioid dosing, preferring to rely on cannabinoids and physical activity. Functional goals were updated and the next follow-up scheduled in four months.

Annual wellness visit for a 39-year-old patient with tension headaches and insomnia at Lakeshore Medical Group in Madison, Wisconsin. The patient experimented with hemp-derived delta-8 gummies and a CBN sleep tincture purchased from a local dispensary, reporting mild benefit for sleep onset. Dr. Okafor counseled on the limited regulation of these cannabinoid products and recommended documenting any THC exposure before upcoming employment screening. The patient is curious about medical cannabis certification but plans to first trial sleep hygiene measures and a CBD-only regimen. We scheduled a follow-up to reassess in two months.
